title: Why Avatar: Fire and Ash Was Oscar-Nominated for Costumes That Don't Technically Exist
text: Costume designer Deborah L. Scott earned an Oscar nomination for her work on Avatar: Fire and Ash, despite the costumes being entirely virtual. Scott has collaborated with director James Cameron since Titanic, for which she won an Academy Award. Scott emphasizes that virtual costume design requires tangible work. I draw my sketches old-school, with a pencil on paper, she explains. The process involves creating physical samples before digitization, a crucial step that distinguishes her approach from typical animated films. The designer stresses the importance of hand-crafted prototypes: If you just hand over a design on paper to a VFX company, they determine the materials without knowing the nuances. Scott works with teams in Los Angeles and Weta Workshop in New Zealand to construct actual garments before they're scanned and modeled digitally. Scott views the nomination as groundbreaking, stating it represents a door opening to a new era for costume design beyond live-action films. The work involves continuous collaboration with VFX teams throughout post-production, allowing her to refine costumes based on performers' movements and the director's evolving vision. The process demonstrates that virtual costumes demand the same artistry and craftsmanship as traditional filmmaking costumes.
